text: The camera provides a view into the left lower retrorectus space, showing the anatomical area involved in the surgical procedure.
The surgeon separates the retrorectus fat from the attenuated posterior rectus sheath, revealing the anatomical layers within the left lower retrorectus space.
The camera view shows the surgical field below the arcuate line, focusing on the anatomical structures in this region.
The video shows the fibers of the posterior rectus sheath below the arcuate line, extending towards the midline and inserting into the linea alba.
The camera view shifts to show the anatomical structures above the arcuate line, focusing on the fibers of the posterior rectus sheath as they approach the midline.
The surgeon incises a small opening through the attenuated posterior rectus sheath, demonstrating entry into the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon completes stage one by fully dissecting the retrorectus fat from the attenuated posterior rectus sheath, revealing the preperitoneal space below the arcuate line.
The surgeon visualizes the left retroactive space, ensuring the entire linea alba is visible before proceeding to cross over.
The surgeon uses the left hand to operate a bipolar cautery device above the scissor area, dissecting tissue and cauterizing visible vessels.
The surgeon crosses over the linea alba to continue the dissection in the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon observes a large hernia in the patient's abdomen, noting that the upper abdomen is clear and free of any visible scars.
The surgeon crosses over the linea alba, utilizing the XI robot's flexibility to navigate the surgical space.
The surgeon crosses over a specific area without using any energy devices, likely manipulating tissue manually or with instruments.
The surgeon uses scissors to make a cold cut incision, revealing preperitoneal fat before proceeding with energy-based dissection.
The surgeon performs a preperitoneal dissection, carefully separating the tissue layers to expose the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies preperitoneal fat in the surgical field, distinguishing it from other types of fat such as omental or mesenteric fat.
The surgeon uses cautery on the ventral side of the posterior rectus sheath, carefully managing the tissue to avoid injury.
The surgeon applies gentle cautery to the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring minimal thermal penetration to avoid injury to any bowel located on the opposite side.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the diastatic fascia, aiming to remove as much adipose tissue as possible from the surgical field.
The surgeon leaves a thin layer of fat visible on the surgical field, highlighting its minimal thickness.
The surgeon avoids aggressive dissection to prevent injury to the diastatic fascia, maintaining a cautious approach near the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon identifies a small hernia in the surgical field, visible as a protrusion through the abdominal wall.
The surgeon continues the crossover maneuver into the preperitoneal space, navigating through the surgical field to access the targeted area.
The surgeon scores the ventral side of the posterior rectus sheath using a cold cutting technique, visibly manipulating the tissue without thermal devices.
The surgeon reaches the site of the hernia, with visible anatomical structures indicating the location within the surgical field.
The surgeon inserts scissors behind the posterior rectus sheath, creating a visible separation between the peritoneum and the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon uses scissors to carefully insinuate between the peritoneum and the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring there is no bowel adhered to the area.
The surgeon presses down with their left hand, causing the posterior rectus sheath to visibly shift or compress, demonstrating the effect of manual pressure on this anatomical structure.
The surgeon applies tension to the posterior rectus sheath, demonstrating how it can be scored and divided without using scissors for cutting.
The surgeon uses a cold cut scissor to carefully dissect near the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring that the bowel, which may be close by, is not inadvertently damaged.
The surgeon approaches the second hernia located in the lower abdomen, preparing to address the larger hernia visually evident in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the change in tissue layers, noting the presence of a very thin peritoneum surrounding the intra-abdominal space.
The surgeon encounters dense scar tissue in the surgical field, which is visually distinct from surrounding tissue layers.
The surgeon is working outside the peritoneum, maintaining careful dissection around the area of the hernia in the lower abdomen.
The surgeon identifies the rectus muscle as a landmark in the lower abdomen, providing orientation within the surgical field.
Below the arcuate line, the surgeon observes the robust linea alba, a thickened fibrous structure visible in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon navigates closer to the rectus muscle, avoiding scar tissue while remaining outside the peritoneum.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers to avoid damaging the anterior fascia while working near the rectus muscle and below the arcuate line.
The surgeon identifies the connection between the sac and the anterior fascia, visually assessing the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to keep the peritoneum intact, avoiding damage to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers to avoid moving anterior to the rectus muscle, ensuring the anterior rectus sheath remains undamaged.
The surgeon encounters bleeding and stops it, revealing bloodstained tissue at a specific layer. The surgeon then proceeds beyond this layer to identify and dissect through the clean, areolar tissue.
The surgeon carefully peeks through the peritoneum, revealing a view into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon identifies the location of the lower, larger hernia within the surgical field.
The surgical field shows extensive scar tissue, which the surgeon is carefully navigating.
The surgeon returns to work on the cephalad portion of the hernia sac, focusing on the easier section of the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to bring down the hernia sac, manipulating the tissue to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon attempts to reduce the larger hernia sac, which is visibly resistant and does not easily retract from its position.
The video shows the left superior edge of the hernia, with visible scar tissue surrounding the area.
The surgeon achieves entry into the abdominal cavity, visible as the hernia sac is manipulated to create an opening.
The surgeon attempts to take down the hernia sac at the edge of the defect, carefully manipulating the tissue surrounding the hernia.
The surgeon uses a scissor to push and scrape against the tissue, breaking through the peritoneum to enter the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon inspects the interior of the abdominal cavity, observing minimal adhesions and assessing the difficulty of removing the hernia sac.
The surgeon divides the peritoneum along the neck of the hernia using surgical scissors, carefully separating the tissue to expose the underlying structures.
The video shows two hernia defects: a larger defect on the right side and a smaller defect on the left side in the suprapubic area.
The surgeon examines the lower defect in the abdominal cavity, assessing the hernia sac, which appears difficult to take down.
The surgeon cuts the peritoneum at the edge of the hernia sac, preparing to further address the hernia defect within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon maneuvers around the hernia, visually inspecting the surrounding tissue and anatomical structures within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon exposes the upper part of the surgical field, revealing a thicker white tissue layer, likely representing dense fibrous tissue or fascia.
The surgeon identifies the anterior fascia and attempts to preserve it while working around the hernia defects visible in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon navigates towards the caudal edge of the hernia defect, focusing on the surrounding tissue structures.
The surgeon reaches the caudal edge of the hernia defect and navigates into the right retro-rectus plane, maintaining dissection within the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon identifies two distinct tissue planes and proceeds to enter the deeper plane, likely within the retro-rectus space, as part of the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon navigates into the space between the retro-rectus fat and the attenuated posterior rectus sheath, carefully advancing through the anatomical layers.
The surgeon cuts through the attenuated posterior rectus sheath, which extends low in this patient, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon demonstrates the dissection extending almost all the way down to the pubis, revealing the anatomical structures in the retro-rectus plane.
The surgeon opens the tissue to reveal the true space of Retzius, a distinct anatomical area located deep within the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates down into the pelvic region, revealing anatomical structures within the space between the retro-rectus fat and the attenuated posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon identifies the right superior pubic rami, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon retracts tissue with the left hand, revealing a layer of cotton candy-like areolar tissue, indicating a usable surgical plane.
The surgeon navigates along the right edge of the defect in the pelvic region, using careful dissection to maintain the appropriate surgical plane.
The surgeon continues working below the arcuate line, where the posterior rectus sheath is not clearly visible.
The surgeon opens the surgical field broadly, carefully dissecting away from their position, revealing underlying tissue layers.
The surgeon identifies a visible plane within the abdominal wall, suggesting an appropriate area for further dissection.
The surgeon observes a visual distinction between the upper and lower fat layers, noting differences in their appearance.
The surgeon maintains dissection within the previously identified plane, ensuring careful navigation along the right edge of the defect while remaining below the arcuate line.
The surgeon carefully dissects and takes down the peritoneum, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues to work along the lower defect, maintaining dissection within the visible tissue plane.
The surgeon observes muscle twitching, providing a visual cue for anatomical orientation during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies attenuated posterior rectus sheath and incises it, entering the retro-rectus space.
The surgeon begins to bring down the posterior rectus sheath, revealing the underlying retro-rectus space.
The surgeon navigates through a different tissue layer on the right side compared to the left side, indicating a change in anatomical depth or plane.

The surgeon points out the distinction between the retrorectus and preperitoneal spaces, cutting through the attenuated transverse salus. On the right side, the dissection occurs in the retrorectus space, while on the left side, it is in the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon remains in the retrorectus space, maintaining the dissection plane within this anatomical layer.
The surgeon continues to bring down the attenuated posterior rectus sheath, revealing the underlying retrorectus space.
The surgeon has completed dissection approximately three centimeters deep into the retropubic space, indicating the depth of the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection in a cephalad direction within the retropubic space, progressing deeper into the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the contralateral posterior rectus sheath and develops the retro rectus space on the contralateral side during stage three of the ETEP dissection.
The surgeon encounters challenging tissue planes while developing the retro rectus space, requiring careful manipulation to separate the layers.
The surgeon uses their left hand to apply traction, exposing aerial or tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum, revealing potential paths for further dissection.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the lower fascial edge, avoiding forceful manipulation as it is deemed unnecessary for the procedure.
The surgeon plans to perform a bilateral transversus abdominis release (TAR), aiming to bring the edges of the posterior rectal sheath together.
The surgeon navigates between the posterior rectus sheath and the rectus muscle, indicating successful entry into the retro-rectus space.
The surgeon uses their left hand to guide the scissors during dissection, navigating the surgical field with precision.
The surgeon carefully dissects approximately one centimeter away from the linea alba, maintaining a safe distance from this central abdominal structure.
The surgeon navigates through a messy surgical field, attempting to preserve the fascia on the anterior portion of the tissue.
The surgeon navigates the retroactive space, maintaining close proximity to the posterior rectus sheath while ensuring that fibroareolar tissue remains attached to the ceiling of the surgical field.
The posterior rectus sheath is visibly retracted, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers along the edge of the fat, tracing the boundary of the posterior rectus sheath with scissors.
The surgeon leaves the neoperitoneum on the edge of the anterior rectus sheath, maintaining its position during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the incision along the posterior rectus sheath, continuing the dissection in the retroactive space.
The surgeon leaves a portion of tissue on the edge of the posterior rectus sheath to provide a secure area for suture placement.
The preperitoneal space is visible on the right side of the screen, showing the anatomical area being accessed during the procedure.
The surgeon continues dissection in the retrorectus space, revealing the anatomical area behind the rectus sheath.
The surgeon develops the preperitoneal space, extending the dissection across to the contralateral linea alba and slightly beyond, connecting the previously dissected spaces.
The surgeon cuts the posterior rectus sheath to join the preperitoneal and retrorectus spaces, creating a single flap visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the peritoneum, visually separating it from the underlying structures within the preperitoneal space.
The surgical view shows the preperitoneal space on the right side and the retrorectus space on the left side, illustrating the spatial relationship between these two anatomical areas.
The surgeon combines the preperitoneal space with the right retroarctus space, visually merging these two anatomical areas.
The surgeon performs additional preperitoneal dissection near the line of division, carefully separating the peritoneum from adjacent tissues.
The surgeon dissects the transverse fascia, moving it downward to further expose the underlying structures.
The surgeon clears the pre-peritoneal fat to facilitate the opening of the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring the peritoneum remains intact without any perforations.
The surgeon approaches the large defect near the xiphoid process, focusing on precise dissection to avoid damaging the nearby peritoneum.
The surgeon uses an instrument to push down the posterior rectus sheath, creating a fibrous tissue layer resembling cotton candy, which facilitates easier dissection.
The surgeon incises the left posterior rectus sheath, using precise movements to separate the sheath from underlying structures.
The surgeon maintains a distance from the linea alba while manipulating the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon ensures hemostasis to prevent bleeding in the area where the transversus abdominis release (TAR) will be performed later.
The surgeon resumes the crossover and epigastric subxiphoid dissection, focusing on the area beneath the xiphoid process.
The surgeon demonstrates a tap dissection technique, focusing on the anatomical area around the left posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon scrapes from the posterior rectus sheath onto the adjacent fat, dragging the transverse salus tissue away to reveal a shiny posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon completes the incision along the posterior rectus sheath, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection up to the xiphoid area, focusing on the epigastric region.
The surgeon uses scissors to divide the posterior rectus sheath, guided by the left hand, maintaining a distance of approximately one centimeter from the linea alba.
The surgeon completes the division of the posterior rectus sheath and opens the retro rectus space by lifting the fibro fatty areolar tissue, staying close to the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon prepares to continue with the transversus abdominis release (TAR) procedure following the completion of the posterior rectus sheath incision.
The surgeon demonstrates the insufflation of the bowel, which appears distended due to the introduction of gas.
